content:
—————


We are zipping towards an ignorant utopia. The world will soon be everything humanity has ever wanted: there will be peace (or almost no violence) and there will be no scarcity. However—this is empty. When we can have anything all of the time: what is then that becomes of value? in a population that is made of consumers, it seems that even in a perfect world there can be no such thing as true happiness, no such thing as being content. the celebrity will become even more valued as the real means of social worth, worshipped as gods. the layperson will consume. The Earth will become uninhabitable, so we will be forced stuck into the confines of a space allotted to us. We will exist entirely on the internet, and real human interaction—physical interaction—will be lost. Procreation will be halted.


The Ancient world has always been of greatest interest to me. I think we are very close to our Ancient brethren in a lot of our thinking and our ambitions as a culture. But, most of the great time periods have such bountiful themes and limits to explore. I want to explore the passing of time through one character, moving through each major time period in the length of the novel. Perhaps, taking one major event from each time period and paralleling it to something the main character has felt/experienced through his life (i.e. the assassination of Caesar, the creativity of Michelangelo, World War I, etc.). The plot of the novel would take place in the moments between the death of the body and the death of the mind.
context: From document: Fiction creates distance from reality, allowing me to try and assume… — chunk 2 (chunk 2/4)
